FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD cohort, 7471 young adolescents (mean age 9.87±0.62 years) from the ABCD Study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models examining intelligence-MRI associations, spatial correlation analysis, mediation analysis with MRI phenotypes and neurotransmitter density maps

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable found in results tables. Step 2: Composite includes "physical activities, screen use and sleep duration" per methods—bundles screen use with non-phone/non-SM items (physical activity, sleep) without separate breakout. Multi-item bundling without phone/SM isolation fails IC2.

notes: Paper analyzes lifestyle factors (physical activity, screen time, sleep) as mediators of intelligence-neural correlations. Screen use examined as part of broader lifestyle composite, not as isolated SM/phone variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study, 11,875 US adolescents at baseline, 8,125 with complete MRI and cognitive data (mean age 9.93 years); 21 recruitment sites across the United States

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effects models examining reading/TV viewing associations with brain morphology (cortical area, thickness) and neurocognitive performance; ROI-based and vertexwise analyses with regional specific effects

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable. Study analyzes daily hours spent reading books and daily hours spent television viewing—TV viewing alone does not qualify as smartphone or social media use per binding clarifications. Reading books is not a phone/SM activity. Step 2: Not applicable; no phone/SM composite present.

notes: Paper focuses on reading books and television viewing in relation to neurocognitive outcomes and brain structure. Neither reading nor TV viewing constitute smartphone or social media use as defined by the v7 criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, prospective longitudinal cohort data from baseline through 3-year follow-up (Y3), adolescents aged 10–14 years, diverse US sample

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: mixed-effects linear regression models examining ACEs exposure (at Y1) and PSMU (at Y3) with socio-demographic covariates; moderation analysis with emotional and behavioral dysregulation

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Issues in Mental Health Nursing

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: PSMU (Problematic Social Media Use) measured via SMAQ at Y3 is a qualifying SM variable per binding list (Bergen/SMAQ-type scales are SM variables). Paired with individual-trait-level outcome: emotional and behavioral dysregulation (BPM Internalizing, Externalizing, Attention, Total Problems scales). Main finding: cumulative ACEs at Y1 associated with higher PSMU at Y3 (β=1.5, p<0.001), examined in relation to emotional/behavioral dysregulation.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: β=1.5, p<0.001 for high ACEs (≥4) vs. low (<2) on PSMU; mixed-effects linear modeling with 95% CI provided throughout; moderation interaction terms tested and reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results: mixed-effects models, regression coefficients with p-values and confidence intervals for ACEs→PSMU association and moderation effects

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies. Paper includes cross-sectional analyses at Y1 alongside longitudinal Y1→Y3 analyses. Design is predominantly longitudinal, but presence of any cross-sectional analysis with PSMU as DV disqualifies FT-EC7 per binding rule.
notes: Paper reports ACEs exposure at Y1 associated with higher PSMU at Y3 (β=1.5, p<0.001). Emotional/behavioral dysregulation measured at Y1; moderation tested but not significant. Meets IC2 (qualifying SM variable PSMU paired with individual-trait outcome), IC3/IC4 (quantitative results), and does not meet FT-EC7 due to cross-sectional carve-out.
